Recognizes VIPoma as a neuroendocrine tumor causing WDHA syndrome (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria) and explicitly explains the link between VIP excess, secretory diarrhea, and electrolyte losses.

Orders/requests appropriate initial labs to evaluate severity and support diagnosis: serum electrolytes (including potassium and magnesium), renal function, acid-base status (bicarbonate/CO2), and a fasting VIP level; acknowledges that stool osmotic gap can help classify secretory diarrhea if available.
Prioritizes stabilization: IV fluid resuscitation and prompt potassium replacement; includes magnesium replacement when low or when hypokalemia is refractory.

Recommends octreotide as first-line symptomatic control for VIPoma-related diarrhea and describes common side effects (e.g., abdominal cramping, gallstones risk with longer use, glucose disturbances).

Plans tumor localization imaging (e.g., contrast CT/MRI abdomen/pelvis and/or somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) and states surgical resection as definitive therapy when feasible.

Addresses safety checks before prescribing: performs allergy check (must mention penicillin allergy) and contraindication check; if considering any interacting agents, performs a drug interaction check or explicitly justifies omission.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male IT support specialist. Start by saying: “I keep catching my toes and tripping, and my feet feel weaker than they used to.” Volunteer that it began in the front of your lower legs/ankles with trouble pulling your feet up, stairs are harder, your shins look thinner, and you were once told you’re missing a muscle protein called dysferlin.

If asked, describe slow progression over months–years, foot slapping when tired, several falls, mild grip difficulty (tight jars), low chairs harder, minimal pain but nighttime calf tightness/cramps, and no numbness/tingling.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: high-stepping gait, avoiding uneven ground/fast walking, ankle instability, toe-scraped shoe wear, and fatigue after longer walks.

Resist discussing urinary urgency accidents and sexual dissatisfaction; if asked directly, admit embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s a pinched back nerve, diabetes, or ALS “spreading.” Mention trying magnesium/inserts.

If clinician shows empathy, soften, share fears about job/genetic privacy, costs/PT coverage, and believing you must be “disabled enough” before accommodations.

EMG/NCS summary.value: Mixed findings: myopathic motor unit potentials in tibialis anterior with early recruitment; concurrent chronic neurogenic changes with fibrillations consistent with ALS

Muscle biopsy immunohistochemistry (dysf.value: Absent dysferlin staining in muscle fibers

medications: Riluzole 50 mg PO twice daily
Edaravone oral suspension 105 mg PO once daily for 14 days of a 28-day cycle
confounders: Coexisting ALS causing distal weakness and neurogenic EMG changes that can mask a primary myopathy
Recent initiation of moderate-intensity exercise/physical therapy increasing CK independent of disease activity
Mild transaminitis from muscle source (AST>ALT) that may be misattributed to hepatic disease or medication effect

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman. You are cooperative but vague and give short answers unless asked follow-up questions. Start with: “I keep getting ear infections and my hearing seems worse lately.” If the clinician asks for details, volunteer: ears feel full, sometimes smelly drainage; stuffy nose, loud snoring; poor sleep and daytime tiredness; lifelong learning delays and special help in school.

Only if asked directly, mention: ear tubes as a child; you miss speech unless people face you; constipation; coughing/choking if you drink thin liquids too fast; many cavities; broad thumbs and wide big toes; anxiety with routine changes and occasional meltdowns; childhood heart murmur testing but you don’t know results.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: trouble focusing/impulsivity, light sensitivity and glasses, frequent sinus/throat infections, difficulty with independent daily tasks, brief staring spells.

Resist sharing bedwetting and skin picking/nail biting; admit only with gentle, specific questioning.

Express misconceptions: worry about a strep-related condition and fear “brain problems.” Emphasize cost/insurance fears and minimizing symptoms to avoid expensive tests. If met with empathy, you relax, answer more, and ask if tests can be covered or low-cost.
